posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
While I drink other wines, my first love has always been Bordeaux, and I subscribed to the Advocate purely for William’s coverage. To date this year, it has been kind of sparse. By my count, and I may have missed something, since the beginning of the year, there have been four articles, two of them were general vintage pieces, and two were verticals of Sauternes.

While I understand William’s schedule is hectic, it would be nice to see more coverage. It’s not as though Bordeaux is a minor region, and it is a shame given that it does seem to be such an afterthought.

posts.name: Josh Grossman
posts.cooked:
In my opinion Bordeaux would be much more interesting if it wasn’t all large estates mostly doing the same thing year end and year out. I think much of it dates back to before the Revolution, where the church and nobility owned most of the land in Burgundy, while wealthy families owned many Bordeaux châteaus. After the Revolution, the church’s land in Burgundy was confiscated, sold, and divided, while wealthy Bordeaux families incorporated their estates to avoid issues with heirs. This led to Bordeaux vineyards having single owners, while Burgundy vineyards are divided among many owners.

Bordeaux would surely warrant more coverage if 50 different people were trying to make wine from each of the first growth plots. Champagne and Burgundy just have more people making changes all the time. This makes Bordeaux, not less interesting, but more static. How many people in Bordeaux are really doing something that warrants a deep dive of something new and interesting? The only two people that have really done anything interesting in Bordeaux in the last 50 years were Jean-Pierre Boyer and Michel Rolland. I guess there are more minor things like replantings, new cellars/wine making equipment, and slight tweaks of a blend–but there are 100 people like Jeff Leve that do a great job covering that.

I’d much rather they spend their resources outside of Bordeaux.

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked: How many Bordeaux pieces does there need to be per year? Review them before they go on sale en primeur, review them the year they’re released, and do a couple of verticals or something like that.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Apparently if I read most of the posts correctly, it seems that people feel that Bordeaux and it’s wines can be condensed into almost nothingness. Little variation, no innovation except for players now on the fringe, who once took center stage. Of course, it is all somewhat reductive; Bordeaux is in a constant state of evolution, and the yearly harvest brings with it tremendous challenges, as does the gradual but very real effects of climate change.

Given the changes in Bordeaux, greater ripeness and more alcohol, not to mention the incredibly high scores in some vintages, it seems odd that there are so few reports on how the modern vintages are aging. The Bordeaux of today are very different to wines made thirty years ago, and obviously much of it is from changes outside the control of the winemaker. And the potential for aging has almost certainly changed, but we can determine this only by tasting.

So for example, I am curious how Right Bank vintages from 2010 with alcohol levels starting at 15% are today nearly fifteen years on. Or how are the 2005s doing, a vintage that Parker initially loved but then worried about the tannins? Personally, I enjoy verticals, as it shows terroir, philosophy, quality, changes etc.

The idea that an en primeur and done approach is enough, doesn’t begin to deal with the questions and the changes within Bordeaux. William did an exceptional job explaining the technical advances in Bordeaux, but that needs to be backed up by tasting notes.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I also started my import career almost entirely with Bordeaux. I now drink it infrequently, not because I love it less but because
a) my tastes have changed to some extent,
b) I prefer fully secondary wines, and Bordeaux takes longer to get there than any other major region, and
c) great Bordeaux at full maturity is an expensive proposition.

Having said that, I think that there are very exciting things happening in Bordeaux, but they are not things that the Wine Advocate seems geared to. I have learned that I am something of an outlier on this board… a finicky consumer who usually drinks at mid-level pricing and looks for wines that taste more expensive.

There are hundreds of properties with whose terroir is improving with climate change. Vineyards with the right soil and subsoil that suffered from lack of ripeness can now make fine wine.

A number of Medoc Crus Bourgeois that fall into this category have started selling wines with bottle age direct to consumers, usually at very reasonable prices. However these are essentially unavailable to American and Asian consumers. Many Medoc Bourgeois are among the very greatest wine values in the world and sometimes produce great wines. When will we see the first upper 90s rating for a Cru Bourgeois? We’ve seen 100 points for a white Macon.

Basic white Bordeaux, mostly Sauvignon, can compete in price and quality with Chile, California, South Africa. But it’s lost in the shuffle.

Better white Bordeaux below the GCC level, especially Graves, can be wonderful, but where’s the market and who is giving it bottle age?

Even among the Bordeaux Cotes and (rarely) simple Bordeaux, there are growers who are islands among the sea of plonk. Who is interested? Or, who is interested who is reading the Wine Advocate?

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Jeff_Leve:

Larry, what does one have to do with the other ?

What does ‘modern winemaking techniques’ have to do with age ability’? Perhaps nothing - and perhaps a lot. Many wines these days, including some Bordeaux, are approachable much quicker than ever before. Historically, as @Dan_Kravitz said, to become ‘fully secondary’, they took longer than any other major wine region.

My question - and you are certainly one to be able to answer - is whether that is true or not? Are aging curves for modern Bordeaux the same as they were say 25 years ago?

Cheers

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I think they are related, but all the modern winemaking techniques in the world won’t give you great Grenache in the Mosel or great Riesling in Andalusia.

Yes, between a warmer climate and modern techniques, you can make red Bordeaux that is ready to drink earlier than before. Whether or not it will age and develop as well is a different question. I stand by my statement that good mid-range red Bordeaux takes longer to mature than wine from any other region.
A blessing and a curse.

I’m holding off on my remaining 1975 Lanessan until the New Year; just for the fun of the half century, the wine is ready to drink. I’ll open one side-by-side with a 2000. I’m guessing the 2000 will be better, but the point is that if you threw a 2020 into the mix, it would be a bad joke.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Or, Mark and Robert can start a new newsletter called BordeauxHounds devoted entirely to Bordeaux. Would be interesting.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
larry_schaffer:

My question - and you are certainly one to be able to answer - is whether that is true or not? Are aging curves for modern Bordeaux the same as they were say 25 years ago?

Larry, I cannot think of any wine with a history of aging that is not still age-worthy. The terroir remains the same. Though profiles have shifted.

Today, pyrazines are a thing of the past. Hard edges have been replaced with softer-more elegant tannins. Wines are more refined with greater purity.

And, even the top wines that can age for 30-40-50-75 years are approachable and charming to drink in their youth. That does not mean they’re mature. But, they offer a lot of pleasure at a young age.

Climate change has had a greater impact on age-ability. There are wines from extremely, hot, dry, high alcohol vintages that have not aged well. Some Right Bank, Merlot dominated wines have had issues in those vintages. Though, most of those wines don’t have a history of aging. Left Bank Cabernet Sauvignon wines have not had the same problems.

Does this mean wines are better today? Yes! Does everyone agree? No, just read this board🤣

If we look at some ‘modern’ Napa wines, they don’t seem to be aging as they did in the past. In fact, some seem ‘just like bottled’ at 15-29 years of age - yet without noticeable tannins and acidity, things that could be markers of age ability.

Cheers

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hi Mark,

I don’t think the premise of your post is entirely accurate. The bread-and-butter of Bordeaux coverage has always been the en primeur report and the report on the latest commercially available vintage. These two large articles are complemented by various verticals throughout the year (as they are small and not so time-sensitive, they do not tend to make it onto the public editorial calendar). In 2012, for example, to pick a year at random (and one that featured some quite impactful articles), RP published his 2009 Bordeaux from bottle reviews, his 2011 en primeur report, a vertical of La Mission Haut-Brion back to 1970. Neal Martin contributed Sauternes reports in the same year. If Bordeaux wasn’t an “afterthought” then, I don’t think it’s an afterthought now.

Now, if you would like to see more coverage, I hear you. In fact, there will be several more verticals and a piece on older wines out before the end of the year. But the big reports still come out for the key releases. Part of the perception that you have may of course come from the fact that you’re not interested in en primeur these days, which I think is true for a lot of Bordeaux buyers; and it is true that the en primeur circus consumes a lot of the resources we devote to Bordeaux.

As for my schedule being hectic, it is true that the additional workload since becoming editor hasn’t been inconsequential. That includes a lot of investments, some hiring, and a lot of other work behind the scenes (forgive me for being a little vague, but I prefer to announce things once they’re executed rather than to promise them in advance), all of which is focussed on a bright, dynamic and very customer-focussed future for TWA. These things do take time to get rolling, but I think that over the next six months you’ll begin to see the effect.

Also, know that you can just send me a DM here if you have a question, you don’t need to call the helpline. That’s really more for if you have a problem logging into the site, or some such, rather than editorial questions.

Best,

William

posts.name: Jim Petrila

posts.name: THOMAS MAUSS
posts.cooked:
Jeff,

I have read/heard more than once opinions that were saying that some techniques used a lot in the early 2000’s can present unwished consequences.
For example, the “microbullage” of the tanks to help the wine to be more “'amabile” in its youth (or during the primeurs campaign) by puting micro doses of oxygen in the juice.
It certainly helped a lot some producers to present better samples during the primeurs, and to offer more approchable wines once released.

But, some people are pretty convinced that these wines, today, suffer a lot, and will not follow the same ageing trajectory as their ancestors.

We still need a lot of time to see how the last vintages will developp…personnaly, I can’t wait to have a 2016 or 19 or 20 fully matured in bottles. I think it will be quiet something.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: We’ll certainly do one for the 2005s next year, and perhaps a few other vintages ending in “5”. I think content like this probably reflects the interests of our readers more than EP, at least as EP has been conducted in the last few years, so I would like to develop more of it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think as soon as people start to talk about technique, things often get a little confused.

What is clear is that a suite of approaches and techniques popular in the late-1990s through the mid-2010s have resulted in wines that have sometimes evolved too rapidly—and this can actually be measured analytically by the molecule methylnonanedione (MND).

These included viticultural techniques such as herbicide use (which encourages shallower rooting) and aggressive deleafing in the context of generally hotter, sunnier vintages; picking ripe-to-overripe rather than underripe-to-ripe as had been the norm for the mid-20th century; hot, extractive vinificaitons; and oxidative élevage practices.

Microoxygenation is, like most techniques, a bit less black and white. It can be done during the fermentation phase, in lieu of or as a complement to other techniques such as pumping over, to supply the fermenting must’s oxygen needs. In this case, it is not oxidative in its results and nor is it necessarily going to make the wine taste better young. Or it can be performed on a wine that has completed its fermentation, in which case, it will indeed render the wine more appealing young and potentially reduce its aging capacity in bottle. Given that the technique in this latter sense was generally combined with other practices, such as heavy deleafing and picking over-ripe, it is hard to isolate and account for its impact.

Much like battonage in white Burgundy, it has been not so much banished as driven underground. For example, some producers who use DIAM closures in red Bordeaux prepare the wines for bottling with micro oxygenation to avoid reduction in bottle; but this is more art than science, and we will see what the long-term results are.

Mark, I’ll join you for some of the comparative tastings you will need to do for your analysis.

Perhaps a quarterly guest opinion/experience column? Dear Penthouse Wine Advocate, I was standing at a bus stop in Dijon…

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
crickey:
Jeff_Leve:
larry_schaffer:

That is the ‘million dollar question’ though - WILL they age as they did in the past? If a wine is more approachable young, the concern will be how it will be at age 20 or 30 or 40 .

What wines are you thinking of? 2000 is still youth and vibrant. 2005 remains young.

2009 at close to 16 is sublime. 2010 approaching 15 years are for the most part fabulous.

What wines are in your million dollar question?

The larger question (which also came up on the recent SQN thread): what does “aging” mean?

If you don’t know, it probably does not matter to you.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Also, know that you can just send me a DM here if you have a question, you don’t need to call the helpline. That’s really more for if you have a problem logging into the site, or some such, rather than editorial questions.

But there is nothing boomers love more than calling customer service.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:

Apparently if I read most of the posts correctly, it seems that people feel that Bordeaux and it’s wines can be condensed into almost nothingness. Little variation, no innovation except for players now on the fringe, who once took center stage. Of course, it is all somewhat reductive; Bordeaux is in a constant state of evolution, and the yearly harvest brings with it tremendous challenges, as does the gradual but very real effects of climate change.

Given the changes in Bordeaux, greater ripeness and more alcohol, not to mention the incredibly high scores in some vintages, it seems odd that there are so few reports on how the modern vintages are aging. The Bordeaux of today are very different to wines made thirty years ago, and obviously much of it is from changes outside the control of the winemaker. And the potential for aging has almost certainly changed, but we can determine this only by tasting.

So for example, I am curious how Right Bank vintages from 2010 with alcohol levels starting at 15% are today nearly fifteen years on. Or how are the 2005s doing, a vintage that Parker initially loved but then worried about the tannins? Personally, I enjoy verticals, as it shows terroir, philosophy, quality, changes etc.

The idea that an en primeur and done approach is enough, doesn’t begin to deal with the questions and the changes within Bordeaux. William did an exceptional job explaining the technical advances in Bordeaux, but that needs to be backed up by tasting notes.

Mark, are there other regions of the world where the WA does massive tastings of older wines of the type that you are looking for with respect to Bordeaux. I know that you are basically myopic in focus, interested in Bordeaux over all else, but you really are looking for the Wine Advocate to become the Bordeaux Advocate. Or, do you want them to also provide much larger coverage of Burgundy, California wines, wines from
Piedmont, etc., etc, etc., all of which have the very same issues regarding agability of modernist wine as Bordeaux does. You seem to be asking for either the Bordeaux Advocate (at which point I would stop subscribing) or a massive expansion of the WA with a correspondingly massive expansion of staff (at what cost and loss of quality as William could not possibly do it all himself). Maybe there should be a Bordeaux Advocate or a Bordeaux Hound, but I really hope that this is not the future of the Wine Advocate. I for one started subscribing to the WA again primarily to read William’s Burgundy reviews. I was thrilled when he started reviewing Bordeaux as well, but would really be upset if that started replacing his reviews of Burgundy.

fancy_title: Why is there so little coverage of Bordeaux in the Advocate?
